annotations.label: Concrete category (informal)

anchor.snippet: a concrete category is a category that is equipped with a faithful functor to the category of sets

annotations.note: `ConcreteCategory C FC` packages a category with a faithful functor `forget : C ⥤ Type` (Faithful instance given immediately in `Forget.lean`).

annotations.label: Concrete category (elementary form)

anchor.snippet: consists of a class of objects , each equipped with an underlying set

annotations.note: The class exposes `ToType X : Type` and `hom : (X ⟶ Y) → ToHom X Y` with `FunLike` coercion, capturing the underlying-set view.

annotations.label: Concrete category (formal)

anchor.snippet: A concrete category is a pair

annotations.note: Defined as a class `ConcreteCategory C FC` carrying the faithful functor data `hom`/`ofHom` with bijection axioms.

annotations.label: Forgetful functor interpretation

anchor.snippet: The functor U can often be thought of as a forgetful functor

annotations.note: `forget C : C ⥤ Type w` is the canonical faithful functor, with `(forget C).Faithful` instance proven.

annotations.label: Homomorphisms in a concrete category

anchor.snippet: It is customary to call the morphisms in a concrete category homomorphisms

annotations.note: Mathlib does not adopt the word "homomorphism" generically; instead `ConcreteCategory.hom : (X ⟶ Y) → ToHom X Y` converts morphisms to bundled functions.

annotations.label: Concretizable category

anchor.snippet: A category C is concretizable if there exists a concrete category

annotations.note: No predicate "Concretizable" exists; only specific `ConcreteCategory` instances are declared.

annotations.label: Every small category is concretizable

anchor.snippet: All small categories are concretizable

annotations.note: The faithful Yoneda embedding `C ⥤ Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Type v₁` is proven, but no theorem packages a small `C` as a `ConcreteCategory`.

annotations.label: Distinct topologies, same underlying set

anchor.snippet: if S and T are two different topologies on the same set X

annotations.note: No declaration exhibits two distinct topologies on the same set as a remark on non-injectivity of `forget TopCat`.

annotations.label: Four-element set with two group structures

anchor.snippet: any set with four elements can be given two non-isomorphic group structures

annotations.note: Mathlib has no statement contrasting `ℤ/4` and `ℤ/2 × ℤ/2` as different group structures on a four-element set.

annotations.label: Category of sets with partial functions

anchor.snippet: the category of sets with partial functions as morphisms

annotations.note: `PartialFun` is defined as the category of types with `Part`-valued partial functions as morphisms.

annotations.label: Group as a concrete category

anchor.snippet: Any group G may be regarded as an "abstract" category

annotations.note: `SingleObj M` realises any monoid/group as a one-object category whose morphisms are elements of `M`.

annotations.label: Poset as a concrete category

anchor.snippet: any poset P may be regarded as an abstract category

annotations.note: Any preorder (hence poset) carries a `SmallCategory` instance with unique morphisms encoding `≤`.

annotations.label: Category Rel of sets and relations

anchor.snippet: The category Rel whose objects are sets and whose morphisms are relations

annotations.note: `RelCat` is the large category of types with binary relations as morphisms.

annotations.label: Set^op embeds in Rel

anchor.snippet: The category Set op can be embedded into Rel

annotations.note: Mathlib has the faithful `graphFunctor : Type u ⥤ RelCat` and `opEquivalence : RelCat ≌ RelCatᵒᵖ`, but does not directly state the `Setᵒᵖ ↪ Rel` embedding.

annotations.label: Opposite of concretizable is concretizable

anchor.snippet: the opposite of any concretizable category C is again concretizable

annotations.note: No theorem deriving a `ConcreteCategory Cᵒᵖ` instance from `ConcreteCategory C` appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Faithful functor from presheaf category

anchor.snippet: If C is any small category, then there exists a faithful functor

annotations.note: Yoneda gives a faithful (in fact fully faithful) `C ⥤ Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Type v₁`, but no faithful functor `[Cᵒᵖ, Set] → Set` is stated.

annotations.label: Banach spaces with unit ball functor

anchor.snippet: the category Ban 1 of Banach spaces and linear contractions

annotations.note: Mathlib has Banach spaces and contractions individually but no category `Ban₁` nor a unit-ball forgetful functor as a `ConcreteCategory` instance.

annotations.label: Category Cat of small categories

anchor.snippet: The category Cat whose objects are small categories and whose morphisms are functors

annotations.note: `Cat` is defined as bundled `Category`-instances with functors as morphisms (file comment notes it is not concrete).

annotations.label: hTop is not concretizable (Freyd)

anchor.snippet: The category hTop , where the objects are topological spaces and the morphisms are homotopy classes of continuous functions, is an example of a category that is not concretizable

annotations.note: Freyd's theorem on non-concretizability of the homotopy category is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: N-ary predicate and N-ary operation

anchor.snippet: a subfunctor of U N is called an N-ary predicate and a natural transformation

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Subfunctor` exists generally, but `N`-ary predicates/operations on a concrete category's forgetful functor are not defined.

annotations.label: Models of signature recover C

anchor.snippet: The category of models for this signature then contains a full subcategory which is equivalent to C

annotations.note: The signature-and-models reconstruction of a concrete category is not present.

annotations.label: Concrete category over X

anchor.snippet: a concrete category over X

annotations.note: Mathlib's `ConcreteCategory` is fixed to `Type w`; relative concreteness over an arbitrary base `X` is not modelled.

annotations.label: Construct

anchor.snippet: a concrete category over Set is sometimes called a construct

annotations.note: The terminology "construct" for a concrete category over Set does not appear in Mathlib.
